title: Initial Calyx to HW conversion.
body:
This is a start to #2892.

The approach taken here is to go for the most literal translation from one IR to the other, without trying to optimize the dataflow. This is pretty much the opposite of what I initially said in #2892, but it is simpler to implement this way. This emits wires and assigns, which allows us to keep in the same mode as the Calyx IR, where every primitive only has results that are assigned and no operands. This works great because we mostly create single writer wires, which are [canonicalized away](https://github.com/llvm/circt/blob/fb2e548b42bc2bdc12af1b52b7193f7ce7576cb4/lib/Dialect/SV/SVOps.cpp#L1142). There is currently only support for primitives, not sub-components.

The conversion uses the dialect conversion framework. The Calyx dialect is legalized into the HW, Comb, Seq, and SV dialects. Each `calyx.component` becomes a `hw.module` with the appropriate ports. The body of the `calyx.control` must be empty, and the body of `calyx.wires` becomes the body of the `hw.module`.

There is a big `TypeSwitch` to convert anything that implements `CellInterface` into the appropriate "core" ops, when possible. A large chunk of Calyx's standard library maps to `comb` directly. Registers and pipelined primitives can use `seq` directly. All the results of the primitive cells are replaced with wires (for inputs to be assigned) or wire reads (for outputs).

This makes it really easy to just lower `calyx.assign` to `sv.assign`, optionally with a `comb.mux` guard.

This isn't ready for review, but I wanted to share it and a somewhat real example. I've been testing with the attached `test-low.txt`, which is taken from this test (which is a 1-d `torch.matmul` aka dot product): https://github.com/llvm/circt/blob/fb2e548b42bc2bdc12af1b52b7193f7ce7576cb4/test/Conversion/AffineToStaticLogic/loops.mlir#L56-L65

I lowered the above with CIRCT and passed that through Calyx's native lowering (using top-down-st). To convert this to HW, I do:

```
circt-opt test-low.txt -lower-calyx-to-hw -canonicalize -cse > test-core.txt
```

This produces `test-core.txt`, which is looking pretty good at 527 lines of IR in the printed form. For reference, the input Calyx IR had 6k lines of printed IR, and before canonicalization and CSE, the output of `lower-calyx-to-hw` was 20k lines of printed IR. I lowered this to System Verilog in `test-out.txt` with the following:

```
circt-opt -lower-seq-to-sv -canonicalize -cse -hw-cleanup -prettify-verilog -export-verilog test-core.txt > test-out.txt
```

This came out pretty neat, at 255 lines of text. Some wires could be removed and expressions inlined, but this seems fairly readable. The source locators mean nothing because I ran circt-opt starting with an MLIR file, but if it was from a real frontend they would be meaningful. For reference, I included `test-native-out.txt`, which is about 26k lines of text, excluding the standard libary. I have yet to test them, but I should be able to hook them up to the same testbench. I haven't taken the time to make the multiplier exactly the same, so they won't be formally equivalent yet, but that could be on the table for testing as well.

There are some TODOs we can address in follow up commits. A good one would be the assertions mentioned in #2892. There are also probably some ops that need coverage, but a lot of the standard ones were either needed or identical to ones that were needed.

[test-low.txt](https://github.com/llvm/circt/files/8601043/test-low.txt)
[test-core.txt](https://github.com/llvm/circt/files/8601044/test-core.txt)
[test-out.txt](https://github.com/llvm/circt/files/8601045/test-out.txt)
[test-native-out.txt](https://github.com/llvm/circt/files/8601046/test-native-out.txt)
